candidates.snippet:
exempt: true,
    rationale:
      'Better Auth device-authorization codes are redirect/device-flow protocol state, not an app read surface under SPEC.md §10.1.',
  },
  invitation: { domain: 'organization', key: 'organizationId' },

findings.title: Open redirect in redirectPath: backslash bypass of the protocol-relative URL check
findings.description: betterAuthSignInEmailMutation (L944-964) and betterAuthSignUpEmailMutation (L998-1019) compute their post-auth destination as `redirectTo: redirectPath(input.next, options.defaultRedirectTo ?? '/')`. The `next` value is fully attacker-controlled: it is declared on betterAuthSignInEmailInput (L154-158) / betterAuthSignUpEmailInput (L160-165) and arrives as a query param or form field (e.g. `/login?next=...`). The resulting `result.value.redirectTo` is emitted verbatim as a 303 `Location:` header by consumers (confirmed in examples/reference/src/app.ts:342 and 374, and app-shell.ts authRedirectTo). The only validation is redirectPath (L1204-1209): `if (!value.startsWith('/') || value.startsWith('//')) return fallback;`. This blocks the protocol-relative form `//evil.com` but NOT the slash-backslash form `/\evil.com`. Per the WHATWG URL spec, browsers normalize backslashes to forward slashes for special (http/https) schemes, so `Location: /\evil.com` is parsed as `//evil.com`, a scheme-relative URL that navigates to `https://evil.com/`. Attack: send a victim `https://victim/login?next=/%5Cevil.com` (or a pre-filled form with `next=/\evil.com`); after a successful sign-in the browser is redirected off-site to the attacker, enabling phishing/credential-harvesting that appears to originate from the trusted login flow. Because redirectPath is a framework-provided helper used internally by the blessed credential mutations, every app relying on the better-auth adapter's `next` redirect inherits this bypass. (Related: redirectPath also returns control characters such as CRLF unchanged when they follow a leading '/', though the Headers layer typically rejects those.)
findings.recommendation: Treat backslashes as slashes before validating, and require the path to start with '/' followed by a non-slash/non-backslash char. E.g. `if (!/^\/[^/\\]/.test(value)) return fallback;` and additionally reject any value containing control characters (\r, \n, \t) or '\\'. More robustly, resolve against a known base with `new URL(value, base)` and confirm the resulting origin equals the app origin before using it.

findings.title: Credential mutations treat any non-{400,401,403} better-auth response as a successful sign-in
findings.description: In the sign-in (L946-964) and sign-up (L1000-1019) handlers, failure is detected only via isBetterAuthCredentialFailureResponse -> isCredentialFailureStatus, which returns true exclusively for HTTP 400/401/403 (L1192-1194). Every other status falls through to `forwardBetterAuthSetCookie(...)` and returns `{ status: 'signed-in'/'signed-up' }` with a redirect. So a 429 (rate-limited), 5xx (better-auth/internal error), or a 200 two-factor-pending response (better-auth returns 200 with a twoFactorRedirect body and no full session cookie when 2FA is required) is reported to the app as a successful sign-in and triggers the post-auth redirect. This is not a direct auth bypass because access still requires the session cookie that better-auth only issues on genuine success, but it produces seriously misleading behavior: users are redirected as 'signed-in' on transient failures, and apps with 2FA enabled would advance past the 2FA UI step. The success path should be positively confirmed, not inferred from the absence of three specific error codes.
findings.recommendation: Detect success positively (e.g. require response.status in the 2xx range AND a session-establishing Set-Cookie) rather than treating 'not 400/401/403' as success. Explicitly handle rate-limit/5xx (surface an error to the user) and the two-factor-pending 200 case instead of redirecting as fully signed-in.
